data.title: Most and least shorted industrial stocks with up to $2B market cap
data.description:
The Electrical Components and Equipment segment within the industrial sector shows a contrasting trend, with companies featuring among both the most and least shorted stocks as of the end of March.

Here are the five most shorted industrial stocks with market capitalizations of up to $2 billion (as a % of shares outstanding)

Robin Energy (RBNE), Short Interest: 30.51%.

Decent Holding (DXST), Short Interest: 30.22%.

Polar Power (POLA), Short Interest: 29.61%.

Eos Energy Enterprises (EOSE), Short Interest: 27.58%.

Enovix (ENVX), Short Interest: 27.50%.

Here are the five least shorted industrial stocks with market capitalizations of up to $2 billion: (as a % of shares outstanding)

Zeo Energy (ZEO), Short Interest: 0.52%.

Rich Sparkle Holdings (ANPA), Short Interest: 0.52%.

Ultralife (ULBI), Short Interest: 0.51%.

DSS (DSS), Short Interest: 0.51%.

Euroseas (ESEA), Short Interest: 0.51%.

data.title: Trump threatens strikes on Iranian infrastructure as conflict escalates
data.description:
U.S. President Donald Trump threatened to target Iran’s bridges and power plants, saying the country’s leadership “knows what has to be done,” and that the United States military would hit Iran “extremely hard” for the next two to three weeks.

The warning comes after an airstrike destroyed a newly built bridge near Tehran, killing eight people, according to Iranian state media.

In a Truth Social post late on Thursday, Trump said the U.S. “hasn’t even started destroying what’s left in Iran. Bridges next, then Electric Power Plants!”

“New Regime leadership knows what has to be done, and has to be done, FAST!” Trump added without elaborating on what needed to be “done.”

In another post on Friday, Trump also suggested the U.S. could “open” the Strait of Hormuz and take control of oil flows.

Iran has effectively halted tanker traffic through the strait after attacks by U.S. and Israeli forces on February 28.

Iranian Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi responded critically, questioning whether Americans wanted to “turn back the clock,” in reference to Trump’s remarks.

Meanwhile, operations at Abu Dhabi’s Hadshan gas facilities were suspended after debris from an intercepted strike caused a fire, with no reported injuries.

Legal experts warned that targeting civilian infrastructure such as power and water facilities could violate international law.

Separately, a proposed U.N. Security Council resolution to authorize  military action against Iran stalled after opposition from China, Russia, and France.

data.title: Quantum risk to crypto is advancing, Google paper warns
data.description:
Google (GOOG) (GOOGL) researchers have issued a new warning that future quantum computers may be capable of breaking the cryptography that protects Bitcoin (BTC-USD) (IBIT) and other cryptocurrencies with fewer resources than previously estimated.

In a recent blog post, Google Quantum AI researchers Ryan Babbush and Hartmut Neven revealed updated estimates showing an approximately 20-fold reduction in the quantum computing hardware needed to crack elliptic curve cryptography—the security foundation underpinning most crypto wallets and transactions.

The researchers said they have compiled quantum circuits that could solve the underlying mathematical problem, known as ECDLP-256, using fewer than 500,000 physical qubits and executing in just minutes. While no such machine exists today, the findings narrow the timeline for when quantum computers might pose a real threat to blockchain security.

“We urge all vulnerable cryptocurrency communities to join the migration to post-quantum cryptography without delay,” the researchers wrote in an accompanying white paper.

Google framed the disclosure as an early warning rather than a prediction of imminent danger, noting that the time remaining before such machines arrive still appears longer than what’s needed to transition public blockchains to quantum-resistant security. However, the “margin for error is increasingly narrow.”

The tech giant recently announced its own timeline to migrate all security systems to post-quantum cryptography by 2029. The company has been working on a responsible transition to quantum-safe standards since 2016.

To share the research responsibly, Google engaged with the U.S. government and developed a zero-knowledge proof method that allows third parties to verify the findings without providing a roadmap for potential attackers.

The researchers highlighted early efforts already underway across the industry, including post-quantum projects on Algorand (ALGO-USD), Solana (SOL-USD), and the XRP Ledger (XRP-USD), calling them evidence that “transition to post-quantum cryptography is realistic.”

data.title: Zoomlion Heavy Industry Science and Technology Co., Ltd. reports FY results
data.description:
• Zoomlion Heavy Industry Science and Technology Co., Ltd. press release (ZLIOF): FY revenue of RMB 52.107B, up 14.58% (YoY).
  • Net profit attributable to shareholders of RMB 4.858B, up 38.01%.
  • International revenue rose 30.52% to RMB 30.515B, accounting for 58.56% of total revenue.

data.title: U.S. Composite PMI falls more than expected in March
data.description:
S&P Global U.S. Composite PMI came in at 50.3 in March, compared to 51.9 in February and the preliminary reading of 51.4, according to data released on Friday.

Services PMI was 49.8, compared to 51.7 in February and vs. 51.1 flash.

Developing... Check back for updates.

data.title: The inflation process has shifted even as headline CPI declined – Federal Reserve
data.description:
Nearly six years after the onset of the COVID-19 pandemic, the underlying inflation process in advanced economies has fundamentally shifted, even as headline consumer price inflation has declined substantially from its 2021–2022 peaks.

According to the Federal Reserve in a recent Fed Note, inflation remains “unusually widespread across categories” despite headline numbers in several economies moving closer to central bank targets.

François de Soyres, chief for Advanced Foreign Economics in the International Finance Division of the Federal Reserve Board of Governors, wrote that price pressures remain elevated across a broad set of goods and services relative to the pre-pandemic period, raising questions about whether this environment reflects a temporary normalization or a lasting change.

The breadth of inflation pressures tells a striking story, he said. At the 2022 peak, “nearly 80% of CPI subcomponents had inflation above 3%,” driven by pandemic-induced demand-supply imbalances.

 

 

 

While this share has declined since then, Fed researchers found it remains “elevated relative to pre-pandemic levels” in all four economies studied—the U.S., the euro area, Canada, and the United Kingdom—with deflation becoming less common across the board.

Perhaps the most significant finding concerns what the authors describe as a weakening relationship between falling prices and aggregate core inflation.

The analysis indicated that “the presence of disinflationary components is doing less to pull down aggregate core inflation than it did before the pandemic,” resulting in persistently positive residuals in their models.

As the researchers explain, this result “reflects a shift in the association between aggregate inflation and the distribution of price changes across categories, suggesting that the underlying inflation process may be evolving.”

Decomposing the data by sector, the research also reveals that non-housing services account for the most widespread price increases across all four economies studied. De Soyres found that a substantial share of industries are experiencing wage growth above 3%, with services particularly affected.

According to the analysis, this “broad-based strength in wage growth may contribute to the persistence and breadth of price pressures documented in earlier sections.”

The report concludes that “inflation is no longer just about temporary shocks to goods or housing” but instead reflects broad-based wage growth contributing to persistent price pressures.

The researchers emphasize that the “composition of price pressures remains different from the pre-pandemic era,” though they caution that more data will be needed to assess whether this represents a lasting change.

Importantly, the authors note this does not imply that central banks cannot achieve their 2% objectives, particularly since the pre-pandemic relationship was associated with inflation that tended to run below target.

More on the U.S. economy

  • Business inventories unexpectedly slip M/M in January
  • ISM Manufacturing PMI unexpectedly rises in March
  • PMI Manufacturing index for March rises marginally less than initially estimated
  • St. Louis Fed's Alberto Musalem can support raising or lowering policy rate if required
  • Retail sales increase more than expected in February

data.title: AerCap Holdings reports solid Q1 with 202 leases, $3B financing boost
data.description:
• On Friday, AerCap Holdings N.V. reported its Q1 2026 results and completed 202 lease agreements, covering 81 aircraft, 102 engines, and 19 helicopters.
  • During Q1, it made 32 purchases, including 10 aircraft, 20 engines, and 2 helicopters.
  • The company also completed 52 sale transactions, including 47 aircraft, 4 engines, and 1 helicopter.
  • Additionally, they signed $3B in financing deals, repurchased ~5.4M shares for ~$745M, and declared a $0.40 per share quarterly dividend.
  • Source: Press Release
